projects.title: Projects

circuit-simulation.title: Interactive Circuit Simulation
circuit-simulation.description: Real-time circuit simulation with curvature-based speed control
circuit-simulation.pageDesc: Path interpolation + Canvas API. A car that follows the track based on real curvature data.

sections.title: Context
sections.body: Started as a way to represent an F1 circuit visually — not a static image, but something with real movement and physics.

sections.title: Problem
sections.body: Make the car follow the track smoothly, with speed that varies based on actual corner geometry — not just a fixed rate.

sections.title: Solution
sections.body: A system of centerline points with distance-based interpolation. Speed is computed from local curvature: tight corners slow the car, straights speed it up.

sections.title: Real-world reference
sections.body: Lap time is calibrated to the actual circuit record — 1:14.637, Michael Schumacher, Ferrari, 2006.

sections.title: What I learned
sections.body: Canvas animation loops, geometry for trajectory systems, and real-time rendering performance in the browser.

fastf1-analysis.title: F1 Telemetry Analysis Tool
fastf1-analysis.description: Real telemetry data analysis using FastF1 — lap times, tyre strategy and pit stop detection from official F1 API data.
fastf1-analysis.pageDesc: Interactive F1 telemetry analysis tool — compare any two drivers from the full grid with lap time evolution, speed trace and pit stop strategy from real race data.

sections.title: Context
sections.body: A self-directed learning project towards F1 software engineering. Using real race data to understand how teams analyze strategy, tyre degradation and driver performance.

sections.title: What it does
sections.body: Features an interactive driver selector with the full 2026 grid. Select any two drivers and instantly compare their lap time evolution and speed trace side by side. Includes automatic pit stop detection, tyre compound labels per stint, team colors and hover tooltips showing exact lap times. Analysis of the 2026 Miami GP.

sections.title: Real-world reference
sections.body: Data sourced directly from the official F1 timing API via FastF1 — the same data feed used by teams during race weekends.

sections.title: What I learned
sections.body: How to work with real telemetry data, detect pit stops programmatically from lap time deltas, and build clear visualizations that tell a story from raw numbers.

roadmapCompleted: Lap time evolution with tyre compound labels
Speed trace — fastest lap telemetry comparison
Interactive driver selector — full grid, any combination
roadmapPending: Multi-GP comparison — same circuit across seasons

netclone.title: NetClone
netclone.description: Automates new router setup when switching ISP — detects the router's IP, opens its admin panel via Playwright, and applies your existing SSID and password so every device reconnects automatically.
netclone.pageDesc: Automates the first thing you do when switching internet provider: configuring the new router with your existing SSID and password, so every connected device reconnects automatically — no manual reconfiguration on any of them.

sections.title: Context
sections.body: When you switch ISP, you get a new router. Every device on your network loses its connection. The fix is simple: give the new router the same SSID and password your devices already know. NetClone automates exactly that.

sections.title: What it does
sections.body: You provide your SSID and password — the credentials your devices already use. NetClone detects the new router's IP from the system routing table, opens its admin panel via Playwright, and applies your credentials using a recipe file specific to that router model.

sections.title: Why local
sections.body: Router admin panels are only accessible from the local network — no cloud, no relay. Everything runs on your machine.

sections.title: Status
sections.body: Work in progress. Network detection is functional. Router automation and recipe system for common ISP routers in progress.
